mnemonics.title: Message Passing Components
mnemonics.content: SARA: Send - Assemble the message, Route through network, Assemble at destination, Receive - deposit in buffer. This captures the end-to-end communication process in distributed memory.

mnemonics.title: Network Topology Properties
mnemonics.content: DIBE: Diameter - measure of worst case latency, In/Out degree - connections per node, Bisection bandwidth - throughput capacity, Edge count - cost metric. Essential topology evaluation criteria.

mnemonics.title: MPI Collective Operations
mnemonics.content: BRAG: Broadcast (one-to-all), Reduce (all-to-one), Allgather (all-to-all with gathering), Gather (one-to-all collection). Common collective communication patterns.

acronyms.usage: A programming abstraction that makes distributed physical memory appear as a shared address space to programmers, combining advantages of both models.
The dominant standard for distributed-memory parallel programming, providing portable primitives for point-to-point and collective communications.
Hardware component that connects each processing node to the interconnection network, handling message encoding/decoding and DMA transfers.
Memory architecture where access time depends on memory location relative to the processor; common in distributed shared memory systems.
Communication between exactly two processes, the fundamental building block of message passing, implemented via MPI_Send and MPI_Recv.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a 3D cube with 8 nodes at corners. Each corner connects to 3 others along edges. For higher dimensions, each new dimension doubles the nodes by creating a mirror copy and connecting corresponding nodes.
Think of latency as the time to start a car (α), and bandwidth as the speed once moving (β). Short trips are latency-dominated; long trips are bandwidth-dominated.
For a 2D grid, draw extra padding cells (ghosts) around each processor's local domain. In each timestep, copy boundary values to neighbor's ghost cells before computation.
Imagine a 4×4 grid where the right edge wraps around to the left edge, and the top edge wraps to the bottom. Each interior node has 4 connections: up, down, left, right.

analogies.analogy: Think of separate offices (processors) with private filing cabinets (local memory). To share information, employees must physically walk or send memos (messages) through a hallway (network). No one can access another's cabinet directly.

Sending a letter versus making a phone call. Latency is like the time to write and mail a letter. Bandwidth is like the conversation speed once connected. Small messages resemble quick notes; large data transfers are like reading a long document over the phone.

At a party where everyone must introduce themselves to everyone else individually. With P people, each person shakes hands with P-1 others, resulting in P×(P-1) total interactions—inefficient but thorough.

Assembly line in a factory. Each station (processor) does one operation and passes partially completed work to the next. Throughput is limited by the slowest stage, but work flows continuously once pipeline is full.
